title: Retry backoff becomes exponential; config key renamed
intent_summary: Replaces the fixed 1s retry delay with capped exponential backoff so a struggling upstream is not hammered at a constant rate, and renames the RETRY_DELAY config key to RETRY_BASE_DELAY to match the new meaning.

threads.title: Exponential backoff
threads.summary: The retry loop's delay policy changes from fixed to exponential with a 60s cap — the behavioral heart of the branch. Read this first: everything else is consequence.

claims.summary: Retries now back off exponentially from RETRY_BASE_DELAY, doubling per attempt, capped at 60s.
claims.detail: The delay computation moves from a constant to base * 2**attempt with an explicit cap; attempt count semantics are unchanged.
challenge_questions: Is the cap applied before or after the doubling — can a single delay exceed 60s?

claims.summary: No jitter: concurrent clients that fail together will retry in lockstep.
claims.detail: Every caller computes the same schedule from the same base, so a thundering herd re-forms at each backoff step. The connection pool shares one retry policy across workers.
challenge_questions: How many concurrent callers share this policy in production?
Would full-jitter change the p99 recovery time here?

evidence.note: the schedule is deterministic per attempt
widened: src/client/pool.py — the pool shares one policy instance

claims.summary: The stated goal asked for jitter on the backoff; nothing in the branch adds it.
claims.detail: The issue names 'exponential backoff with jitter'; the new schedule is deterministic. Either the omission is deliberate (worth saying where) or the goal is not fully served.
challenge_questions: Was jitter deliberately deferred, or missed?

evidence.note: the schedule has no random component

claims.summary: Run the retry timing tests and check one pins the 60s cap.
challenge_questions: Does any test fail if the cap is removed?

evidence.note: tests/test_retry.py exists but the diff does not touch it

threads.title: Config key rename (drive-by)
threads.summary: RETRY_DELAY becomes RETRY_BASE_DELAY. Mechanical, but it is a public config surface — compatibility is the question, not the code.

claims.summary: Deployments setting RETRY_DELAY silently lose their override — the old key is neither read nor warned about.
challenge_questions: Should the old key be honored for one release with a deprecation warning?

claims.summary: README still documents RETRY_DELAY; nothing in the diff updates it.
challenge_questions: Is the key documented anywhere else (deploy templates, runbooks)?

evidence.note: README.md is not among the changed files
